name: Btwn Spider Lake & Lost Land Lake
short_description: Explore glacial deposits and lake-edge rockhounding between two scenic northern Wisconsin lakes. Hunt for quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens in this flat, accessible terrain.
description:
This rockhounding location sits in the heart of Wisconsin's glacially-carved lake country, positioned between Spider Lake and Lost Land Lake in Sawyer County. The area offers relatively flat terrain at approximately 1395 feet elevation, making it accessible for rockhounds of varying skill levels. The lakes and surrounding landscape were shaped by Pleistocene glaciation, which deposited diverse mineral-rich glacial drift across the region. You can expect to find quartz fragments, feldspar specimens, and mica flakes among the glacial till and along shoreline areas where wave action has exposed fresh mineral deposits.

The flat landscape here contrasts with other Wisconsin rockhounding areas and provides easy walking access to prime collecting zones. The lake-edge environment is particularly productive after spring thaw or heavy rains, when water movement exposes new material. The combination of glacial deposits and natural stone sorting by water makes this an ideal beginner to intermediate site.

This region receives moderate visitation from local rockhounds, and the public access between the two lakes offers good collecting opportunities without excessive crowding. The northern Wisconsin setting means relatively cool summers and significant winter snowfall, which affects seasonal access and comfort.
history: This area lies within Wisconsin's glaciated terrain, shaped by the Laurentide Ice Sheet during the Pleistocene epoch. The glaciers deposited mixed mineral assemblages including feldspar, quartz, and mica derived from Canadian shield rocks and local bedrock, creating the glacial till and outwash deposits that rockhounds collect here today. The lake basins themselves were carved by glacial erosion, and continued wave action continues to expose fresh mineral material along the shorelines.
tips: Search the shorelines and banks between the two lakes, especially in areas where recent erosion or wave action has exposed lighter-colored glacial deposits - these concentrations often contain the best quartz and feldspar specimens. Use a small hand rake or cultivator to sift through the upper 6-12 inches of soil and glacial drift where minerals are most accessible. Look for translucent to milky quartz pieces, flesh-colored feldspar crystals, and thin mica sheets; the best specimens are typically 0.5-2 inches in size. Bring a small mesh screen or sieve if you plan to do serious sifting, as this speeds up material sorting considerably.
terrain_notes: Flat to nearly level terrain at 1395 feet elevation. The landscape features mixed forest with occasional clearings, sandy/gravelly glacial soils, and shoreline access to both lakes. Vegetation is typical northern Wisconsin boreal-transition forest - birch, aspen, pine, and spruce. Walking distances from parking to prime collecting areas are typically under 0.5 miles. Footing is generally stable but can be muddy or boggy in low areas during wet seasons. Shorelines may be rocky or sandy depending on location.
best_season: May - September. This window avoids deep snow and harsh cold of Wisconsin winters while providing accessible terrain and pleasant collecting conditions. Late May through August offers the warmest, most comfortable conditions. Early fall (late August-September) can also be excellent with fewer insects and mild temperatures.
written_directions: From the Town of Spider Lake area in Sawyer County, head toward the public access points between Spider Lake and Lost Land Lake. These lakes are located approximately 15 miles north-northwest of Hayward. Follow County Road M north from Highway 77 toward the Spider Lake area. Access points are typically available at public boat launches and shoreline areas. GPS coordinates (46.102982, -91.17652) will guide you to the center of the rockhounding area between the two lakes. Roads are paved to the main access points, with potential gravel or dirt road segments depending on which specific lake access point you choose.
road_conditions: Paved roads to main lake access points. Some segments may be gravel or unpaved. Standard passenger vehicle accessible to primary parking areas.
parking_notes: Public access points typically include boat launch areas with parking. Capacity varies by location. Multiple access points available between the two lakes provide alternatives if one area is crowded.
collection_rules: This location appears to be public access near state waters in Wisconsin. Wisconsin state law permits rockhounding on public lands for personal use. Respect posted property lines and private land ownership. Collect only what you can carry. Do not remove specimens from protected archaeological or ecological areas. Follow Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources guidelines for public land use.
rules: Personal use collection only. Respect private property and posted boundaries. Do not damage vegetation or disturb shoreline ecosystems. Pack out all trash. Check local regulations and DNR rules before collecting.
nearest_services: Hayward, Wisconsin - approximately 15 miles south. Full services including gas, food, lodging, and supplies.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain at shoreline and surrounding areas is accessible to most hikers and visitors with normal mobility. No significant elevation gain. Shoreline footing can be uneven with rocks or roots, requiring sturdy footwear. Some access points may have rough parking areas. Not suitable for wheelchairs due to uneven natural terrain, but highly accessible for families with children and casual rockhounders.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding between Spider Lake and Lost Land Lake in Wisconsin?
faq.answer: The primary specimens found here are quartz, feldspar, and mica - all part of the glacial deposits left by Pleistocene ice sheets. Quartz pieces range from translucent to milky white, feldspar appears flesh-colored to white, and mica forms thin shimmering sheets. Most specimens are small fragments and crystals 0.5-2 inches in size, making this ideal for beginners learning to identify common minerals.

faq.question: Is rockhounding free between Spider Lake and Lost Land Lake, or do I need a permit?
faq.answer: Rockhounding is free at this public access location - no permit or fee is required for personal use collection. The area provides public access to shorelines and surrounding land managed for recreation. Check Wisconsin DNR regulations before visiting to ensure compliance with current state rockhounding rules.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding near Hayward, Wisconsin at this location?
faq.answer: Late May through September offers the best rockhounding conditions. Water levels are moderate, weather is pleasant (60-75 degrees F), and insects are manageable. Early fall (late August-September) provides excellent collecting with fewer bugs and cooler temperatures. Winter snow makes access difficult, and spring can be muddy.

faq.question: How difficult is rockhounding at Spider Lake and Lost Land Lake for beginners?
faq.answer: This is an excellent beginner location. The terrain is flat and easy to navigate, specimens are relatively abundant, and the minerals found here (quartz, feldspar, mica) are easy to identify. The lake-edge environment provides natural sorting that concentrates minerals, making finds more likely. Families with children aged 6+ will find good success here.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding between the two lakes?
faq.answer: Bring a small hand rake or cultivator for sifting through glacial drift, a mesh screen or sieve to separate larger stones from mineral fragments, and a collection bag. Wear sturdy hiking boots for shoreline walking, bring water and sun protection, and consider binoculars for spotting exposed deposits from a distance. A small magnifying glass helps identify minerals on-site.

faq.question: Are dogs allowed rockhounding between Spider Lake and Lost Land Lake in Sawyer County?
faq.answer: Dogs are generally welcome on public lands and shorelines in Wisconsin, making this a dog-friendly rockhounding destination. Keep dogs under control, respect any wildlife protection areas, and pack out waste. The flat terrain and easy access make this a great location for family rockhounding trips with pets.

meta_title: Spider Lake & Lost Land Lake Rockhounding - Wisconsin
meta_description: Hunt quartz, feldspar, and mica between two scenic lakes near Hayward, Wisconsin. Free public access, easy terrain, perfect for families and beginners.
